topics.title: Polymorphism in Java
topics.description: Polymorphism means 'many forms' — one interface, many implementations. In Java it comes in two flavours: compile-time (method overloading — same name, different parameter lists, resolved at compile time) and runtime (method overriding — subclass replaces parent's method, resolved at runtime via dynamic dispatch). Runtime polymorphism is the cornerstone of OOP: code written against a supertype works correctly for any subtype, enabling open/closed extensibility.
topics.story: Imagine a universal TV remote. Its 'play' button works on a Samsung, Sony, or LG TV — each TV responds differently internally (different firmware, codecs), but the button does the right thing on all of them. That's runtime polymorphism: the remote (your code) calls play(), and the actual TV model (the object type) determines what happens. Compile-time polymorphism is like a café where 'make me a drink' means different things depending on whether you say 'tea', 'coffee', or 'juice' — the barista knows which recipe to use from the words you say upfront.
topics.analogy: Runtime polymorphism = a shape-sorter toy. The slot (method call in code) accepts any shape. You push in a circle — it goes through the circle hole. You push in a square — it goes through the square hole. The slot doesn't care which shape; each shape knows its own hole. Compile-time polymorphism = a printer with two buttons: 'print document' and 'print photo' — same machine, different operations selected at configuration time.
topics.memoryAnchor: COOL mnemonic — Compile-time = Overloading (different param list, picked by compiler). Runtime = Overriding (same signature, child replaces parent, JVM picks via vtable). Golden test: Animal a = new Dog(); a.sound() — compile type = Animal (reference type), runtime type = Dog (actual object). JVM always runs the ACTUAL object's method. Casting rule: Upcasting = automatic and safe (Dog to Animal). Downcasting = manual and risky (Animal to Dog) — always use instanceof first. Poly = many forms of the SAME method name.
topics.firstPrinciples: Without polymorphism: every method that works with Animals would need an if-instanceof chain for Dog, Cat, Bird. Adding a new animal breaks every existing method. With polymorphism: write code against Animal; the JVM dispatches to the correct subclass method via the vtable at runtime. Adding a new animal class requires zero changes to existing code — this is the Open/Closed Principle.
topics.bruteForce: Without polymorphism: `void makeSound(Object animal) { if (animal instanceof Dog) ((Dog)animal).bark(); else if (animal instanceof Cat) ((Cat)animal).meow(); else if (animal instanceof Bird) ((Bird)animal).chirp(); }` — fragile, must edit on every new animal addition.
topics.optimizedApproach: With polymorphism: `void makeSound(Animal a) { a.speak(); }` — works for every Animal subclass now and in the future. The JVM's vtable dispatches to the right speak() implementation. No if-chains, no casting, no code changes when new subclasses are added.
topics.whenToUse: Use runtime polymorphism (overriding) when: different subtypes share a method signature but each has unique behaviour, and you want code to work on all subtypes through a common reference. Use compile-time polymorphism (overloading) when: one logical operation makes sense for different parameter types or counts (e.g., `print(int)`, `print(String)`, `print(double)`). Avoid overloading for unrelated operations — it creates confusion.

examples.title: Runtime Polymorphism — Dynamic Dispatch
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":32,"lineCode":"Animal[] zoo = { new Dog(\"Rex\"), new Cat(\"Whiskers\"), new Parrot(...) };","variables":{"zoo[0]":"Dog@0x100","zoo[1]":"Cat@0x200","zoo[2]":"Parrot@0x300"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Array of Animal references pointing to Dog, Cat, Parrot heap objects. Compile-time type is Animal; runtime types differ"},{"line":36,"lineCode":"makeItSpeak(a);","variables":{"a":"Dog@0x100"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"First iteration: a points to Dog. JVM looks up Dog's vtable, finds Dog.speak(). Calls it → 'Rex: Woof!'"},{"line":36,"lineCode":"makeItSpeak(a);","variables":{"a":"Cat@0x200"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Second iteration: a points to Cat. JVM finds Cat.speak() → 'Whiskers: Meow!'"},{"line":36,"lineCode":"makeItSpeak(a);","variables":{"a":"Parrot@0x300"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Third iteration: a points to Parrot. JVM finds Parrot.speak() → 'Polly: Polly wants a cracker'"},{"line":37,"lineCode":"a.breathe();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"breathe() not overridden — JVM finds Animal.breathe() for all three. Same behaviour shared"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[makeItSpeak Animal ref] --> B{vtable lookup at runtime}
    B -->|ref points to Dog| C[Dog.speak: Woof]
    B -->|ref points to Cat| D[Cat.speak: Meow]
    B -->|ref points to Parrot| E[Parrot.speak: phrase]
    F[a.breathe] --> G[Animal.breathe: same for all]
examples.pseudocode:
COMPILE TIME:
  Animal ref = new Dog()   -- ref type = Animal (compiler sees)

RUNTIME:
  ref.speak()              -- JVM checks: what type is the OBJECT?
  object is Dog            -- go to Dog's vtable entry for speak()
  call Dog.speak()         -- polymorphic dispatch
examples.explanation: The compiler sees Animal references — it checks that speak() exists on Animal. At runtime, the JVM reads the actual object's type tag (Dog, Cat, Parrot) and jumps to that type's vtable entry for speak(). This one method makeItSpeak(Animal) correctly handles every Animal subclass without knowing them at compile time. Adding a new Hamster class requires zero changes to existing code.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring's ApplicationContext.getBean() returns Object / interface type. Spring calls your @Component's methods polymorphically — ApplicationListener, BeanPostProcessor, DisposableBean. Jackson's JsonSerializer<T> — Jackson stores a Map<Type, JsonSerializer<?>> and dispatches to the right serializer at runtime via polymorphic lookup.

examples.title: Compile-Time Polymorphism — Method Overloading
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":32,"lineCode":"p.print(42);","variables":{"literal":"42=int"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Compiler sees int literal 42. Selects print(int) at compile time. No runtime lookup needed"},{"line":33,"lineCode":"p.print(3.14);","variables":{"literal":"3.14=double"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Compiler sees double literal. Selects print(double)"},{"line":34,"lineCode":"p.print(\"hello\");","variables":{"literal":"String"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Compiler sees String. Selects print(String)"},{"line":35,"lineCode":"p.print(1, 2);","variables":{"arity":2},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Two int args — compiler selects print(int, int)"},{"line":38,"lineCode":"p.print((double) 5);","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Explicit cast to double. Compiler selects print(double). Without cast, print(int) would be chosen"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[p.print args] --> B{Compiler resolves at compile time}
    B -->|int arg| C[print int]
    B -->|double arg| D[print double]
    B -->|String arg| E[print String]
    B -->|int int| F[print int int]
    B -->|String int| G[print String int]
    H[Resolution rules]
    H --> I[1. Exact match]
    H --> J[2. Widening primitive]
    H --> K[3. Autoboxing]
    H --> L[4. Varargs]
examples.pseudocode:
OVERLOAD RESOLUTION (compile time):
  1. Find all methods with matching name
  2. Eliminate those with wrong arity
  3. Pick best match by parameter types
     a. exact type match wins
     b. widening conversion (int→long→float→double)
     c. autoboxing (int→Integer)
     d. varargs (last resort)
  4. If ambiguous → compile error
examples.explanation: Method overloading is resolved entirely at compile time using the static types of the arguments. The JVM stores overloaded methods as separate bytecode methods with mangled names (name + descriptor). No dynamic lookup occurs. Return type alone cannot distinguish overloads — only parameter count and types matter.
examples.realWorldUse: Java standard library uses overloading heavily: System.out.println(int), println(String), println(boolean), println(Object). Arrays.sort(int[]), sort(Object[]), sort(T[], Comparator<T>). String.valueOf(int), valueOf(char), valueOf(boolean). All compile-time resolved — zero runtime overhead.

examples.title: Polymorphism with Interfaces
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":30,"lineCode":"for (Drawable d : shapes) { d.color(); d.draw(); }","variables":{"d":"Circle@0x100"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"d is Drawable reference, points to Circle. d.color() → no override in Circle → Drawable.default returns 'black'. d.draw() → Circle.draw()"},{"line":30,"lineCode":"d.color(); d.draw();","variables":{"d":"Square@0x200"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"d points to Square. d.color() → Square overrides it → returns 'blue'. d.draw() → Square.draw()"},{"line":36,"lineCode":"for (Resizable r : shapes) r.resize(2.0);","variables":{"r":"Circle@0x300"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"r is Resizable reference. r.resize(2.0) → Circle.resize() → radius *= 2. Circle viewed through two interfaces simultaneously"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    Circle -->|implements| Drawable
    Circle -->|implements| Resizable
    Square -->|implements| Drawable
    Square -->|implements| Resizable
    
    renderAll -->|List of Drawable| Circle
    renderAll -->|List of Drawable| Square
    doubleAll -->|List of Resizable| Circle
    doubleAll -->|List of Resizable| Square
examples.pseudocode:
INTERFACE POLYMORPHISM:
  List<Drawable> shapes   -- hold any Drawable implementor
  d.draw()                -- dispatched to actual type
  d.color()               -- dispatched: default OR override

ONE OBJECT, MULTIPLE TYPES:
  Circle IS-A Drawable AND IS-A Resizable AND IS-A Object
  Can be held in List<Drawable> OR List<Resizable>
examples.explanation: Interfaces enable polymorphism without class inheritance. A Circle can be treated as a Drawable by renderAll() and as a Resizable by doubleAll() — the same object viewed through different interfaces. Default methods provide shared implementations that can be overridden. This decouples the code from concrete types: add any new Drawable without changing renderAll().
examples.realWorldUse: Java Collections framework: List, Set, Map are interfaces — code written against List<E> works with ArrayList, LinkedList, or any custom implementation. Spring: JpaRepository interface — your code depends on the interface; the framework injects the concrete implementation at runtime. Comparable, Runnable, Callable — all enable polymorphic dispatch through interfaces.

examples.title: Polymorphic Collection Processing
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":36,"lineCode":".mapToDouble(Taxable::finalPrice)","variables":{"item":"Food@Apple"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"For Food: finalPrice() = basePrice()*(1+taxRate()) = 2.00*1.05 = 2.10. Uses Food's taxRate() via polymorphism"},{"line":36,"lineCode":".mapToDouble(Taxable::finalPrice)","variables":{"item":"Electronics@Laptop"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"For Electronics: 1000.00*1.18 = 1180.00. Same finalPrice() default method, different taxRate() override"},{"line":36,"lineCode":".mapToDouble(Taxable::finalPrice)","variables":{"item":"Luxury@Watch"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"For Luxury: 500.00*1.28 = 640.00. The default finalPrice() calls THIS object's taxRate() polymorphically"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[Taxable::finalPrice] --> B[calls this.taxRate]
    B -->|Food instance| C[0.05]
    B -->|Electronics instance| D[0.18]
    B -->|Luxury instance| E[0.28]
    C --> F[2.00*1.05=2.10]
    D --> G[1000*1.18=1180]
    E --> H[500*1.28=640]
examples.pseudocode:
DEFAULT METHOD + POLYMORPHISM:
  finalPrice() defined once in Taxable
  calls this.taxRate() -- polymorphic!
  each class provides its own taxRate()
  finalPrice() automatically uses the right rate

SUM: cartTotal works for any Taxable now and forever
examples.explanation: The default interface method finalPrice() calls this.taxRate() — and 'this' is the actual runtime object, so it polymorphically dispatches to the correct taxRate() override. One default implementation, three different behaviours. The Stream pipeline processes all items uniformly — the correct tax is applied to each without any instanceof logic.
examples.realWorldUse: Tax engines in fintech: one TaxCalculable interface, dozens of product category implementations. Payment processing: Chargeable interface with finalCharge() default method calling virtualizedRate() — credit card, PayPal, BNPL each override the rate. Spring Security's AccessDecisionVoter: vote() called on a list of voters polymorphically; each voter decides yes/no/abstain.

examples.title: Overloading Pitfalls — Autoboxing and Widening
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":19,"lineCode":"d.call(prim);","variables":{"prim":5},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"prim is int. Compiler finds call(int) — exact match. No boxing needed."},{"line":20,"lineCode":"d.call(boxed);","variables":{"boxed":"Integer(5)"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"boxed is Integer. Compiler finds call(Integer) — exact match. No unboxing needed."},{"line":27,"lineCode":"d.call((long) prim);","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Explicit cast to long. Compiler selects call(long)."},{"line":28,"lineCode":"d.call((Object) boxed);","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Explicit upcast to Object. Compiler selects call(Object) — most general."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[Overload resolution priority] --> B[1. Exact match]
    B --> C[2. Widening primitive int→long→float→double]
    C --> D[3. Autoboxing int→Integer]
    D --> E[4. Varargs]
    F[Widening beats autoboxing!]
    G[null fits all reference types → ambiguous if multiple match]
examples.pseudocode:
RESOLUTION PRIORITY:
  int arg + call(int) available   → call(int) exact
  int arg + only call(long)       → widen: call(long)
  int arg + only call(Integer)    → autobox: call(Integer)
  int arg + call(long) + call(Integer) → widening wins

RUNTIME type of ARGUMENT does NOT matter for overload:
  Animal a = new Dog();
  method(a) → resolves to method(Animal), NOT method(Dog)
examples.explanation: Overload resolution uses the compile-time type of arguments, not the runtime type. This is the opposite of method overriding. Priority: exact match > widening > autoboxing > varargs. A widening conversion (int to long) is preferred over autoboxing (int to Integer). Passing null is ambiguous when multiple reference-type overloads exist — compile error. This is why Josh Bloch's Effective Java advises avoiding overloading for methods that differ only in these ways.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's Collections.synchronizedList(List<T>) vs Collections.synchronizedList(Collection<T>) — same name, different params, resolved at compile time. Pitfall: ArrayList.remove(int index) vs remove(Object o) — remove(1) removes index 1; remove(Integer.valueOf(1)) removes the value 1. This ambiguity has caused production bugs.

problems.title: Polymorphic Area Calculator
problems.description: Create abstract `Shape` with abstract `double area()`. Extend as `Circle(double r)`, `Rectangle(double w, double h)`, `Triangle(double b, double h)`. Implement `double totalArea(Shape[] shapes)` that sums all areas polymorphically — no instanceof, no casting.
problems.hint1: totalArea loops over the array calling `s.area()` — each Shape's override is called.
problems.hint2: Circle: `Math.PI * r * r`. Rectangle: `w * h`. Triangle: `0.5 * b * h`.
problems.hint3: Store all three types in a `Shape[]` array — widening assignment is implicit.
problems.constraints: No instanceof in totalArea.
problems.editorial: s.area() is a polymorphic call — the JVM dispatches to Circle.area(), Rectangle.area(), or Triangle.area() based on the actual object. totalArea never needs to know the concrete types. Adding a new shape (Pentagon) requires no changes to totalArea.

problems.title: Predict Overloaded Method Call
problems.hint1: `show(5)` — int literal → exact match `show(int)`.
problems.hint2: `show(5L)` — long literal. No exact match. long widens to double. `show(double)`.
problems.hint3: `show('A')` — char widens to int. `show(int)`.
problems.constraints: Compile-time resolution only.
problems.editorial: 5 → int (exact). 5.0 → double (exact). "hi" → String (exact). 5L is long — no show(long), widens to double. 'A' is char — widens to int (char→int widening is standard). Widening order: byte→short→int→long→float→double.

problems.title: Interface Polymorphism — Sound System
problems.description: Create interface `Playable` with method `play()`. Implement `MP3Player`, `VinylPlayer`, `StreamingPlayer` — each with a unique play() message. Write `playSong(Playable p)` that calls `p.play()`. Show all three players work through the same method.
problems.hint1: `interface Playable { void play(); }`
problems.hint2: Each class implements Playable and overrides play() with its own message.
problems.hint3: `playSong(Playable p) { p.play(); }` — one method, three behaviours.
problems.constraints: playSong must not use instanceof.
problems.editorial: playSong depends on the Playable interface, not any concrete class. Any class implementing Playable can be passed. This is the Dependency Inversion Principle — depend on abstractions, not concretions.

problems.title: Overloaded add() Methods
problems.description: Implement a `Calculator` class with overloaded `add()` methods: `add(int, int)`, `add(double, double)`, `add(int, int, int)`, `add(String, String)` (concatenation). Demonstrate all four and verify each resolves to the correct version.
problems.hint1: Return the correct type from each: int, double, int, String.
problems.hint2: `add(int, int, int)` uses three parameters — different arity.
problems.hint3: Call `add(1, 2)`, `add(1.5, 2.5)`, `add(1,2,3)`, `add("Hello ","World")` and print results.
problems.constraints: All four overloads in the same class.
problems.editorial: Overloading allows the same logical name for different input types. Compiler selects based on argument types and count at compile time. Each overload is an independent method in bytecode with a unique signature (name + parameter descriptor).

problems.title: Polymorphic toString() in Collection
problems.description: Store a `Dog`, `Cat`, and `Bird` (all extending `Animal`) in a `List<Animal>`. Each class overrides `toString()` with a unique string. Iterate the list and print each — observe polymorphic dispatch through toString().
problems.hint1: Each subclass overrides toString() returning e.g. `"Dog named Rex"`.
problems.hint2: System.out.println(animal) implicitly calls animal.toString() — polymorphic.
problems.hint3: Store all in `List<Animal>` and use a for-each loop.

testCases.input: Dog Rex, Cat Whiskers, Bird Tweety
testCases.output:
Dog named Rex
Cat named Whiskers
Bird named Tweety

problems.constraints: No instanceof in the print loop.
problems.editorial: println(obj) calls obj.toString(). Since each subclass overrides toString(), the correct version is dispatched at runtime. This is polymorphism through Object's toString() — the most common form in Java logging and debugging.

problems.title: Strategy Pattern Using Polymorphism
problems.description: Implement a `Sorter` interface with `sort(int[] arr)`. Create `BubbleSorter`, `SelectionSorter`, `InsertionSorter` implementations. Write `sortAndPrint(int[] arr, Sorter strategy)` that uses whichever strategy is passed. Swap strategies at runtime.
problems.hint1: interface Sorter { void sort(int[] arr); }
problems.hint2: Each implementation sorts the array in-place with its algorithm.
problems.hint3: sortAndPrint(arr, new BubbleSorter()) vs sortAndPrint(arr, new InsertionSorter()) — same method, different behaviour.
problems.constraints: Do not use Arrays.sort inside the implementations.
problems.editorial: Strategy pattern: polymorphism allows swapping the sorting algorithm at runtime without changing sortAndPrint. This is the Open/Closed Principle — sortAndPrint is closed for modification but open for extension (new strategies). Used in Java's Comparator, Spring's TransactionManager, SLF4J's Logger implementations.

problems.title: Varargs Overloading
problems.description: Implement `sum(int... nums)` (varargs) and demonstrate it's called for 0, 1, 2, 3 arguments. Also add `sum(int a, int b)` (exact 2-arg) and show which version is called for exactly two arguments. Explain the resolution in comments.
problems.hint1: Exact match always beats varargs: `sum(int, int)` wins over `sum(int...)` for two int args.
problems.hint2: Varargs is the last resort: `sum(5)` → `sum(int... nums)` since no exact `sum(int)` exists.
problems.hint3: Varargs: `int sum(int... nums) { int t=0; for(int n:nums) t+=n; return t; }`
problems.constraints: Both overloads must exist.
problems.editorial: Varargs is the lowest priority in overload resolution — tried only after exact match, widening, and autoboxing all fail. For two int args, sum(int, int) is an exact match and wins. For 0, 1, 3 args only sum(int...) matches.

problems.title: Polymorphic Factory Method
problems.description: Implement a `NotificationFactory` where `createNotification(String type)` returns an `Email`, `SMS`, or `PushNotification` — all implementing `Notification.send(String message)`. The caller never knows the concrete type. Show polymorphic dispatch through the factory.
problems.hint1: interface Notification { void send(String message); }
problems.hint2: Factory: `switch(type) { case "email" → new Email(); case "sms" → new SMS(); ... }`
problems.hint3: Caller: `Notification n = createNotification("email"); n.send("Hello");`
problems.constraints: Caller code must not use instanceof.
problems.editorial: Factory + polymorphism: the factory encapsulates object creation; the caller works through the interface. When a new channel (WhatsApp) is added, only the factory changes — all calling code stays the same. This is the Factory Method pattern and Open/Closed Principle.

problems.title: Overloading vs Overriding — Predict Output
problems.hint1: For `a.go("hi")`: compile-time type of `a` is `A`. A only has `go(Object)`. Compiler resolves to go(Object). Runtime: B's override of go(Object) is called.
problems.hint2: For `b.go("hi")`: compile-time type of `b` is `B`. B has go(Object) AND go(String). String matches go(String) exactly → compile-time picks go(String).
problems.hint3: Overloading = compile-time (reference type). Overriding = runtime (object type).
problems.constraints: Conceptual prediction.
problems.editorial: The key insight: overload resolution uses COMPILE-TIME reference type. A only knows go(Object), so that's picked at compile time — then at runtime B's override is dispatched. B knows both go(Object) and go(String); String matches go(String) exactly, so that's picked at compile time and called at runtime.

problems.title: Covariant Return in Polymorphic Chain
problems.description: Create a `Vehicle` class with `Vehicle withColor(String color)` that returns this (for chaining). Extend as `Car` with `Car withColor(String color)` — covariant return — and `Car withWheels(int n)`. Show that chaining works without casting when using a `Car` reference.
problems.hint1: Car.withColor() returns Car (not Vehicle) — covariant narrowing.
problems.hint2: With Vehicle ref: `vehicle.withColor("red")` returns Vehicle — can't chain `.withWheels(4)` without casting.
problems.hint3: With Car ref: `car.withColor("red").withWheels(4)` — compiles because withColor returns Car.
problems.constraints: No casting when using Car reference.
problems.editorial: Covariant return: Car.withColor returns Car (a subtype of Vehicle). This lets Car references chain withWheels() without casting. Vehicle references still compile (covariant return is backwards-compatible) but you can't chain Car-specific methods without a cast.

problems.title: Observer Pattern — Polymorphic Notification
problems.description: Implement a simple Observer pattern: `EventListener` interface with `onEvent(String event)`. Register multiple listeners (Logger, Emailer, Auditor) in an `EventBus`. When `bus.publish(event)` is called, all listeners are notified polymorphically.
problems.hint1: EventBus holds `List<EventListener> listeners`.
problems.hint2: publish(String event): iterate listeners and call listener.onEvent(event).
problems.hint3: Each listener implementation prints its own message format.

testCases.output:
LOG: user.login
EMAIL: user.login
AUDIT: user.login

problems.constraints: No instanceof in EventBus.publish().
problems.editorial: Observer pattern = polymorphic notification. EventBus calls onEvent() on every registered listener polymorphically. Adding a new listener type (SMSAlerter) requires only creating the class and registering it — zero changes to EventBus. This is exactly how Spring's ApplicationEventPublisher and Java's EventListener framework work.

problems.title: Polymorphic Command Queue
problems.description: Create a `Command` interface with `execute()`. Implement `PrintCommand(String text)`, `AddCommand(List<Integer> list, int val)`, `ClearCommand(List<Integer> list)`. Queue commands in a `List<Command>` and execute them all in order. Demonstrate undo-ability by reversing the queue.
problems.hint1: interface Command { void execute(); }
problems.hint2: Each command captures its target in its constructor — closure over state.
problems.hint3: Execute: `for (Command c : queue) c.execute();`
problems.constraints: No instanceof in the execution loop.
problems.editorial: Command pattern: each command encapsulates an action as an object. The queue holds polymorphic Command references. Execute loop calls execute() on each — correct action dispatched at runtime. Used in Java's Runnable/Callable, database transaction scripts, undo-redo systems, macros.

problems.title: Polymorphic Comparator
problems.description: Create a `Person(String name, int age)` class. Implement three `Comparator<Person>` instances: `BY_NAME`, `BY_AGE`, `BY_NAME_THEN_AGE`. Use Collections.sort() with each to sort a list of Person objects. Print the sorted order for all three.
problems.hint1: BY_NAME: `Comparator.comparing(p -> p.name)` or manual `p1.name.compareTo(p2.name)`.
problems.hint2: BY_AGE: `Comparator.comparingInt(p -> p.age)`.
problems.hint3: BY_NAME_THEN_AGE: `BY_NAME.thenComparingInt(p -> p.age)`.
problems.constraints: Use Comparator API, not manual comparison.
problems.editorial: Comparator is a functional interface — its compare() method is polymorphically dispatched. Passing different Comparator implementations to sort() changes behaviour at runtime. Comparator.thenComparing() chains comparators. This is polymorphism through functional interface dispatch.

problems.title: Double Dispatch — equals() Pattern
problems.description: Demonstrate double dispatch: `equals(Object)` uses a single dispatch (goes to the receiver's equals). Show why `Cat.equals(Dog)` can return true if poorly written (just compares a common field). Fix it using `getClass()` check for strict type equality.
problems.hint1: Broken equals: compares only `name` without checking class — a Cat named Rex equals a Dog named Rex.
problems.hint2: Fix: `if (getClass() != o.getClass()) return false;` ensures same class.
problems.hint3: Show: `new Cat("Rex").equals(new Dog("Rex"))` → true (broken) vs false (fixed).
problems.constraints: No constraints.
problems.editorial: instanceof in equals allows a subclass to equal a parent or sibling class — violates symmetry. getClass() == o.getClass() enforces strict type equality. Effective Java recommends instanceof for sealed hierarchies (where you control all subclasses) and getClass() for open hierarchies. Always override hashCode() when overriding equals().

problems.title: Polymorphic Decorator Pattern
problems.description: Implement the Decorator pattern for a `TextFormatter`. Base: `PlainText(String text)` implements `Formatter` with `format()` returning the text as-is. Decorators: `BoldDecorator`, `UpperCaseDecorator`, `PrefixDecorator(String prefix)` — each wraps another Formatter and adds transformation. Chain: `new BoldDecorator(new UpperCaseDecorator(new PlainText("hello")))`.
problems.hint1: interface Formatter { String format(); } — all decorators implement this.
problems.hint2: Each decorator holds a `Formatter inner` and calls `inner.format()` first.
problems.hint3: BoldDecorator: return `"**" + inner.format() + "**"`. UpperCase: return inner.format().toUpperCase().
problems.constraints: Each decorator must delegate to inner.format().
problems.editorial: Decorator pattern: each wrapper implements the same interface and delegates to an inner Formatter. Polymorphism makes the chain work — each format() calls the next, adding behaviour layer by layer. Infinitely composable without new classes. Java I/O streams (BufferedReader wraps FileReader wraps InputStream) use this exact pattern.

problems.title: Runtime Type Identification Without instanceof
problems.description: Implement a visitor-like mechanism where each `Shape` knows how to accept a `ShapeVisitor` that computes the perimeter. `ShapeVisitor` has `visit(Circle)`, `visit(Square)`, `visit(Triangle)`. Each shape's `accept(visitor)` calls the right visit(). Compute perimeter for a mixed array without instanceof.
problems.hint1: interface ShapeVisitor { double visit(Circle c); double visit(Square s); double visit(Triangle t); }
problems.hint2: Each shape: `double accept(ShapeVisitor v) { return v.visit(this); }`
problems.hint3: PerimeterVisitor: Circle = 2πr, Square = 4s, Triangle = a+b+c (use equilateral: 3*side).
problems.constraints: No instanceof in the main loop or PerimeterVisitor.
problems.editorial: Double dispatch: `s.accept(pv)` dispatches on the Shape type (first dispatch). Inside accept, `v.visit(this)` dispatches on the visitor's method matching the concrete type (second dispatch). No instanceof needed. Adding a new visitor (AreaVisitor) requires only a new class — shapes unchanged.

problems.title: Polymorphic Pipeline — Transform Chain
problems.description: Create a `Transformer<T>` interface with `T transform(T input)`. Implement `UpperCaseTransformer`, `TrimTransformer`, `ReplaceTransformer(String from, String to)`. Build a `Pipeline` that holds a `List<Transformer<String>>` and applies them in sequence. Show that the output is the composed transformation.
problems.hint1: `interface Transformer<T> { T transform(T input); }`
problems.hint2: Pipeline.apply(String s): loop through transformers, each time reassigning `s = t.transform(s)`.
problems.hint3: Chain: trim → upper → replace.
problems.constraints: Pipeline must work for any number of steps.
problems.editorial: Each step in the pipeline is a polymorphic Transformer. apply() dispatches to each step's transform() at runtime. Adding a new step type requires only a new class — Pipeline unchanged. This is how Java's Stream API and function composition work: each intermediate operation is a polymorphic step in a pipeline.

problems.title: Overloading with Null — Ambiguity Fix
problems.description: Show that `process(null)` is ambiguous when both `process(String s)` and `process(Integer i)` exist. Fix by casting: `process((String) null)`. Also show how adding `process(Object o)` resolves the ambiguity as a fallback. Return which version is called in each scenario.
problems.hint1: null fits both String and Integer (both are reference types) → ambiguous → compile error.
problems.hint2: Cast: `process((String) null)` explicitly selects process(String).
problems.hint3: Adding process(Object) provides a least-specific fallback that null resolves to.
problems.constraints: No ambiguous calls — all must compile.
problems.editorial: null matches any reference type. When multiple overloads match, the compiler picks the most specific (smallest in the type hierarchy). String and Integer are both subtypes of Object but neither is a subtype of the other — ambiguous. Solution: always cast null explicitly. Effective Java: avoid overloading with types that can be confused with null.

problems.title: Open/Closed Principle via Polymorphism
problems.description: Implement a report generator that initially supports Text and HTML report formats. Demonstrate that adding a Markdown format requires ONLY creating a new class — no changes to the existing ReportGenerator. The generator calls `format.render(content)` polymorphically.
problems.hint1: interface ReportFormat { String render(String content); }
problems.hint2: TextFormat, HtmlFormat, MarkdownFormat each implement render() with different wrapping.
problems.hint3: ReportGenerator.generate(String content, ReportFormat fmt) calls fmt.render(content) — no change needed for new formats.
problems.constraints: ReportGenerator must not be modified when adding MarkdownFormat.
problems.editorial: Open/Closed Principle: open for extension (new ReportFormat implementations), closed for modification (ReportGenerator never changes). This is polymorphism's killer feature — systems designed around interfaces grow by addition, not modification. Used in Spring's MessageConverter, Jackson's SerializerFactory, JDBC drivers.

problems.title: Polymorphic Recursive Tree Printer
problems.description: Create a composite tree where `TreeNode` can be a `Leaf(String value)` or `Branch(String name, List<TreeNode> children)`. Both implement `void print(String indent)` polymorphically. Print the tree with indentation — each level indented by two spaces more than the parent.
problems.hint1: interface TreeNode { void print(String indent); }
problems.hint2: Leaf.print: `System.out.println(indent + value)`.
problems.hint3: Branch.print: print name then iterate children calling `child.print(indent + "  ")`.

testCases.output:
root/
  A/
    A1
    A2
  B/
    B1
  C

problems.constraints: No instanceof in Branch.print().
problems.editorial: Composite pattern: Leaf and Branch both implement TreeNode. Branch.print() calls child.print() polymorphically — the JVM dispatches to Leaf.print() or Branch.print() as appropriate, enabling natural recursion. No instanceof needed — each node knows how to print itself. Used in XML/JSON parsers, file system trees, UI component hierarchies (Swing, JavaFX).
